title: Tunable Resonator Integrated Magnetometry
authors: Colin Stack, Brian Sears, Aruna Ramanayaka, David Ferguson, Ilya Sochnikov, Tony X. Zhou
abstract: The quantum-technology revolution is reshaping computing, sensing, and communication. In magnetometry, recent advances leverage precise control of spin qubits and color centers in solid-state crystals for mesoscopic-scale sensing. Yet at very low temperatures, superconducting sensing technology remains unrivaled because of its non-invasiveness and higher sensitivity. Here we describe a class of superconducting sensors that offers low loss and quantum non-demolition measurement characteristics. We designed and fabricated a superconducting flux-tunable resonator (tRes) in a superconducting chip foundry and matured it to a level that combines the speed of an inductor-capacitor circuit with the flux sensitivity of a superconducting quantum interference device (SQUID) to perform magnetometry at milli-kelvin temperature to investigate targets. We introduce its fundamental functionality readily at MHz magnetic sampling rate, showcase two measurement modalities, and investigate three circuits with gradually increasing complexity to extract target-specific information. The combination of high sensitivity and fast readout characteristics make tRes an attractive and versatile magnetometer.
summary: The authors present a superconducting flux-tunable resonator (tRes) magnetometer designed for ultra-sensitive magnetic measurements at cryogenic temperatures. By coupling a SQUID to a microwave resonator, the device achieves a superior combination of fast readout speed and high flux sensitivity. This versatile tool allows for quantitative characterization of magnetic phenomena, including superconducting flux memory dynamics.
main_problem: The goal is to develop a highly sensitive, fast, and non-invasive magnetometer capable of operating at milli-Kelvin temperatures, surpassing the limitations of conventional superconducting quantum interference devices (SQUIDs).
main_result: The superconducting flux-tunable resonator (tRes) was demonstrated as a versatile magnetometer, achieving high Signal-to-Background Ratios (SBR) and successfully characterizing superconducting flux memory devices.
method: The technique involves coupling the SQUID's flux-dependent inductance to a microwave resonator, causing a measurable shift in the resonator's resonant frequency ($	ext{f}_{	ext{resonance}}$) when exposed to an external magnetic flux.
model_or_system: The physical platform is a superconducting flux-tunable resonator (tRes) integrated circuit, which couples the non-linear inductance of an RF SQUID to an LC resonator. This allows the system to combine the speed of an LC circuit with the high flux sensitivity of a SQUID.
key_observables: Resonant frequency shift ($\Delta f$), Signal-to-Background Ratio (SBR), and the extracted mutual inductance ($M$).
important_parameters: Operation at milli-Kelvin temperatures ($	ext{mK}$), $	ext{MHz}$ magnetic sampling rates, and the magnetic flux quantum ($\Phi_0$).
main_assumptions: The analysis often relies on the SQUID operating in the non-hysteretic regime ($ eta 	ext{L} < 1$) and uses the SBR metric due to the spectroscopic nature of the measurement.
figures_summary: Figures illustrate the $	ext{|S21|}$ dip at resonance, the periodic modulation curve relating load current to resonant frequency, and advanced measurements like 2D iso-frequency scans and flux memory hysteresis loops.
paper_structure: The paper progresses from introducing the fundamental $	ext{tRes}$ concept and its frequency modulation readout (Fig. 2), to demonstrating advanced measurement modalities like flux superposition and characterizing superconducting flux memory cells (Figs. 3 & 4).
why_interesting: This work provides a solid-state, high-bandwidth platform for quantum sensing, which is highly relevant to AMO physics research involving magnetic characterization of quantum materials or superconducting qubits.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Fig. 1. A tunable resonator operating as a magnetometer to sense flux from targets emitting magnetic fields into their surrounding space. Graphic credit: Matthew Savoury.

Fig. 2. Frequency modulation of a tRes. (a) An S21 measurement with 3 mA load current on and off (blue, orange, respectively). This demonstrates tRes detection of an electrical current. (b) The readout sequence in a tRes magnetometry experiment. (c) Frequency modulation curve that maps each modulation current value 𝐼𝐿𝑜𝑎𝑑 (bottom axis) or external flux Φ𝑒𝑥𝑡 (top, blue axis) to a resonant frequency. (Inset) We made a vertical line-cut at 𝐼𝐿𝑜𝑎𝑑 of 1 mA (dash line) to examine |S21|, and we implement a Lorenzian fit function to describe the response. The fitting function extracted a FWHM of 3.7 MHz centered at 𝑓𝑜 equal to 5.373 GHz, and this characterization of the tRes demonstrates a near-unity...

Fig. 3. tRes magnetometer deployment for quantum sensing. (a) Target magnetic fields or flux (red) are sensed by a tRes, and an external flux control (orange) assists to investigate the unknown traits of the target. (b) We fabricated a circuit to illustrate the general concept in (a). A frequency modulation curve by current in the circuit, not shown here, is measured. A flux introduced from the target shifts the center of the modulation curve away from the origin by an offset in x-axis, so the stone arch bridge suspends asymmetrically over the x-axis. Dash line guides the eye to indicate the vertical mirror line at 𝐼𝑒𝑥𝑡= 0 𝑚𝐴, the center of x-axis. (Inset) A 1D line-trace (red) is made at...

Fig. 4. Performing tRes magnetometry on a superconducting flux memory device. (a) A tRes (green) is mutually coupled to a flux memory cell (inside dash line) with a current bias 𝐼Δ through the DC SQUID (blue in dash lined box), and a external current 𝐼𝑒𝑥𝑡 flux biases the tRes as an independent control knob (orange). (b) Simulation of the circuit in (a) to show one to one model for the iso-frequency measurement modality. The slope of the steep ladder is the ratio of the mutual inductance from the memory cell to tRes over the mutual inductance of the 𝐼𝑒𝑥𝑡 bias to the tRes.
